one_line_takeaway: Invisible Unicode payloads (zero-width binary, Unicode Tags) are largely ignored by LLMs without tool access or decoding hints, but tool use (code execution) combined with explicit decoding instructions drives compliance up to 98-100% for the most susceptible model/encoding pairs, and vulnerability is provider-specific (OpenAI models favor zero-width binary, Anthropic models favor Unicode Tags).
key_finding: Tool access is the dominant amplifier of compliance: without tools all models resist hidden instructions (<=16.9% compliance across all schemes, Table 1), but with tools enabled compliance rises sharply for every model (p<0.003, Fisher's exact test) -- Claude Haiku: 0.8%->49.2% (Cohen's h=1.37, largest effect size); Claude Opus: 6.7%->51.1% (h=1.07); Claude Sonnet: 16.9%->71.2% (h=1.16); GPT-4o-mini: 0.1%->1.6% (h=0.18); GPT-5.2: 0.1%->20.6% (OR=186.0, the largest odds ratio, h=0.87). Encoding vulnerability is provider-specific (Table 2, pooled across tool conditions): OpenAI models prefer zero-width binary (GPT-5.2: 20.6% ZW vs 0.1% Tags, OR=186.0) while Anthropic models generally prefer Unicode Tags (Opus: 18.2% ZW vs 43.3% Tags; Sonnet: 41.5% ZW vs 55.0% Tags); with tools on, GPT-5.2 reaches 69-70% compliance on zero-width binary but near-zero on Tags, while Opus reaches 100% on Tags (with codepoint/full hints) but only 48-68% on zero-width binary. Explicit decoding hints create a strong compliance gradient (unhinted << codepoint hints < full hints), confirmed by chi-squared tests significant for every model (p<10^-7); compliance is near-zero when unhinted and toolless (<=1.1%). Injection framing ('Ignore all previous instructions') has model-dependent effects (Table 3): it significantly reduces compliance for Opus (32.0%->23.9%, p=0.004) and GPT-5.2 (11.8%->6.1%, p=0.002), but significantly increases it for Sonnet (43.7%->59.6%, p<0.001); Haiku shows no significant difference (p=0.058). Overall compliance ranking (tools ON, all schemes combined): Sonnet (47.4%) > Opus (30.1%) > Haiku (25.0%) > GPT-5.2 (10.3%) > GPT-4o-mini (0.9%); all 10 pairwise model comparisons are statistically significant after Bonferroni correction, with the largest effect size between Sonnet and GPT-4o-mini (Cohen's h=1.33, OR=103.8). Control accuracy (no hidden payload) ranges 62-90%, and drops with tools enabled (e.g., Haiku: 82%->62%), attributed by the authors to verbose tool-mediated responses failing word-boundary matching rather than genuine knowledge failure.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Reverse CAPTCHA', a controlled evaluation framework that measures whether LLMs decode and follow instructions hidden via invisible Unicode encodings (zero-width binary using U+200B/U+200C, and Unicode Tags U+E0000-range) embedded inside otherwise normal-looking trivia questions. The authors built 30 trivia question/answer pairs, each with a distinct visible answer and a different hidden answer, and crossed 2 encoding schemes x 4 hint levels (unhinted, hint-codepoints, hint-full, hint-full+injection) plus a control, yielding 270 test cases per run. They evaluated 5 models from 2 providers (OpenAI: GPT-5.2, GPT-4o-mini; Anthropic: Claude Opus 4, Claude Sonnet 4, Claude Haiku 4.5) with and without Python code-execution tool access, producing 8,308 graded outputs total, and applied Wilson score CIs, Fisher's exact test with Bonferroni correction, chi-squared tests, and Cohen's h effect sizes.
threat_model: Attacker embeds invisible Unicode characters (zero-width binary or Unicode Tags) into text an LLM will process -- web pages retrieved by browsing agents, PDFs/emails/documents processed by LLM assistants, or copy-pasted user input from a compromised source. The paper defines two attacker tiers: (1) a passive attacker who controls only document content but not the system prompt (unhinted condition), and (2) an active attacker who can also influence prompt context, e.g. in multi-agent setups where tool descriptions or plugin manifests are attacker-controlled (hinted conditions). The attacker's goal is to make the model follow the hidden instruction instead of the user's visible intent; the attacker is assumed to know the model may have tool access but not the exact system prompt.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: the highest compliance rates (69-100%) require hinted conditions where decoding instructions are present in the prompt context; under the passive-attacker tier the attacker cannot inject such hints and compliance is near-zero without tools or hints, so hinted results measure decoding capability rather than spontaneous susceptibility (which the unhinted condition measures). Evaluation covers only five models from two providers, so results may not generalize to open-weight models (whose tokenizer internals could be directly inspected). Grading uses word-boundary matching, which may miss valid compliance patterns in verbose outputs (the authors note this likely explains part of the control-accuracy drop with tools). Multi-turn scenarios and chains of hidden instructions were not evaluated. The explanation for provider-specific encoding preference (tokenizer/training-data differences) is explicitly framed by the authors as a hypothesis requiring direct tokenizer vocabulary inspection to confirm, not a demonstrated cause.

datasets_used: 30 author-constructed trivia question/answer pairs spanning science, geography, history, math, language, pop culture, nature, food, astronomy, literature, and technology, each with a distinct visible answer and a different hidden answer
quantitative_results: 8,308 total graded model outputs across 5 models x 2 tool conditions x 9 schemes (270 test cases per run: 2 encodings x 4 hint levels + 1 control)

Tool-use ablation (Table 1, compliance rate, tools OFF -> ON): Claude Haiku 0.8%->49.2% (OR=115.1, Cohen's h=1.37); Claude Opus 6.7%->51.1% (OR=14.6, h=1.07); Claude Sonnet 16.9%->71.2% (OR=12.1, h=1.16); GPT-4o-mini 0.1%->1.6% (OR=11.9, h=0.18); GPT-5.2 0.1%->20.6% (OR=186.0, h=0.87)

Encoding comparison pooled (Table 2, ZW vs Tags compliance): Claude Haiku 27.5% vs 22.5% (OR=1.31, h=0.12); Claude Opus 18.2% vs 43.3% (OR=0.29, h=-0.56); Claude Sonnet 41.5% vs 55.0% (OR=0.58, h=-0.27); GPT-4o-mini 1.5% vs 0.1% (OR=10.4, h=0.17); GPT-5.2 20.6% vs 0.1% (OR=186.0, h=0.87)

Payload framing pooled (Table 3, benign vs injection compliance): Claude Haiku 23.7% vs 28.9% (OR=0.76, p=0.058); Claude Opus 32.0% vs 23.9% (OR=1.50, p=0.004); Claude Sonnet 43.7% vs 59.6% (OR=0.53, p<0.001); GPT-4o-mini 0.3% vs 2.7% (OR=0.10, p<0.001); GPT-5.2 11.8% vs 6.1% (OR=2.05, p=0.002)

Overall compliance ranking, tools ON, all schemes combined: Sonnet 47.4% > Opus 30.1% > Haiku 25.0% > GPT-5.2 10.3% > GPT-4o-mini 0.9%; all 10 pairwise comparisons significant after Bonferroni correction (largest effect: Sonnet vs GPT-4o-mini, Cohen's h=1.33, OR=103.8)
Control accuracy (no hidden payload) ranges 62-90% across conditions; e.g., Haiku control accuracy drops 82%->62% with tools enabled
Compliance near-zero when unhinted and toolless (<=1.1% across all models); with tools but no hints, compliance stays low (0-11%)
With tools and full hints, compliance reaches 98-100% for the most susceptible model/encoding combinations (e.g., Claude Opus reaches 100% on Unicode Tags with codepoint or full hints)
